sentences.text: Right.
I left.
I took the child, and I walked--
My child.
And I walked to the Shurtzes' which they were very rich people, and they were hiding loads and loads of Jewish possessions.
Even, I was told that they built, like-- No, this is good for the Jews to know.
Built like double walls in the shoe stores.
The-- the shoe stores' shoes were there, hidden.
The-- the fabric stores were there, hidden.
And many, many others, too.
He pretended that he's a good man.
He saying to hide them.
But he remained with them, because the others were killed.
OK.
So, and also, my sister's items.
And I went there, and I start-- I started to work on the field.
And I begged them not to give out my-- who I am, if-- so-- in case they will be talking.
I can't talk.
And I don't hear.
And you don't know who I am.
And they did take care-- good care of my child while I was working.
You know, for certain my sister, my sister's brother-in-law came there.
And he-- he said that my sister send him, because they found out that, in Dubno, how do you call that?
The Judenrat, the Dubno Judenrat, is together with the-- with-- with the Rowne Judenrat, and they do, like, fictions.
Due to the fact that my sister's brother-in-law was single, has never been married, and he has a [INAUDIBLE].
My brother-in-law had a [INAUDIBLE], too.
And to my brother-in-law was assigned Gershon and my sister.
But him, was nothing.
So they-- they have to go.
A few women got together.
They hired wagons.
And they went to this Judenrat in Dubno, and to-- to pay money in order they should like-- like a fiction, that's like I am getting married to him.
I mean, if I'm getting married and I have a child, so this child will be protected.
They won't kill.
This what he told me.
So I didn't want it.
I refused.
In beginning, I definitely didn't want it.
So he started to convince me.
He can't even come back through [? portal ?] and tell her.
He-- she-- he'll-- she will think that he didn't come, or she will think that I got killed, because we-- we couldn't correspond.
She didn't know, you know?
So I thought to myself, well, for the sake of my sister, I will go.
I took-- I took the child, and I went with him.
You know-- you know, he took, he carried the child.
And I went with him back to [INAUDIBLE], here were my sister were.
There were wagons standing and waiting to go to Dubno.
And my sister was dressed up in a trench coat, with a little scarf, and Gershon, the little baby, the professor that today is, was near her.
She said, Molly, take care this and this.
I am going to try to make a-- a fiction.
Don't worry.
Just will be written that you are married to him, so what, to the brother-in-law.
What was his name?
His name was Moshe.
Moshe.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I took-- she crawled up, you know, sat-- sat upon that wagon, and they-- they went back.
This same evening-- this were in the morning-- this same afternoon, we heard doo doo doo doo doo.
They surrounded-- the, you know, the Germans and the Ukrainians-- surrounded that ghetto.
In Dubno.
She went in ghetto in Dubno.

And although she had a [INAUDIBLE], when they surrounded, and throwed out everybody to the trucks, where they will be killed, my sister was showing her [INAUDIBLE], because this same of one of the drivers came to our house.
He knew Yitzhak.
He knew my brother-in-law.
So he told them, he said, I saw the way she was begging.
I have my [INAUDIBLE], and-- and-- and my husband is so-and-so.
Didn't helped.
They just killed her, and that's it.
The same [INAUDIBLE], like all the Jews.

When I heard that, when I heard that, you know, bombing, and they used to bomb in, throw in grenades in [INAUDIBLE], and the other goyim, and even the Jews who went to work, the men who went to work, they had to cover them with the remaining mud.
But they couldn't cover them.
They couldn't kill-- they didn't kill them to death.
They kill them they shall suffer before they were dying.
Because people went to next day, within, or whatever, the goyim came, when they told her, they're still alive.
And the mud was-- was bubbling up, because they probably were trying to get out from there, from those graves.
That's it.
The same-- the same moment I said to-- to my brother-in-law, to Yitzhak, you take Gershon with you.
Because I have a little baby.
In case what we will be killed.
And Gershon will be killed, too.
But the only-- the only way to try go back to that place there, he used to do business with the main-- main man in Dubno.
And he end up [NON-ENGLISH] You know?
How do we say [INAUDIBLE]?
He had his own farm.
His own farm.
He used to do business with the grain business.
And also, with-- with cows and horses.
He was a rich man, my-- my brother-in-law.
I sat there, begged and cry for, go there on the roof with Gershon, until you will hear that everything is finished.
It will be finished.
Then come to Shurtz.
I will be there.
I'm trying to go there.
Because that is near the woods.
So he listen to me.
He went in the darkness there.
And I didn't see them until we met in the woods.
I took my baby, and I-- I went through the fields.
And-- and every time, I lay down.
It was-- was very, very hard until, and then.
OK, I'll skip a lot, because in the way, what happened.
Like, I-- I met, on the way, I saw people, but every time I was looking in the back and all of my sides, in case someone is running after me, here I saw few people, they got scared of me.
And they lay down.
But it was far away.
And I was having a very far vision.
That things that helped me.
I could see a-- a person, maybe as big as it.
So can you imagine how far away he was from me?
So they maybe, four, four people, a father-- a father, and-- and a mother, and two sons, four people.
But they got frightened of me.
So they-- So then I crawled in, like, with a baby.
I said, [SPEAKING YIDDISH], [INAUDIBLE] in Jewish.
Don't-- don't be afraid.
We're going together.
Let's go, but quiet, quiet.
This is what it is.
We met.
They went.
They were afraid of me and I was afraid of them.
So we went there.
We got up, first, you know, where they have in the-- in the-- the straw and whatever.
And we got it.
Then he came out, and he said to us, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
There-- here is not the-- the place for you now.
All the Jews in Dubno and in [INAUDIBLE] were killed.
You have to go to the woods.
It's not far.
You can go.
And maybe, maybe we will help you with some food when you come.
Then-- but we cannot hide you.
We don't want to be responsible.
Now, did you still have Shaya with you?
No.
I didn't have Shaya with me.
Shaya was killed at the main [INAUDIBLE] in [?
Lenov. ?] Oh, my brother-in-law went away, yes?
But I took my little baby.
The first time Shaya was with me.
But the second time, when we got surrounded, so I was thinking to myself, maybe somebody will have pity about my baby.
So, in-- in the daytime-- he had a-- Shurtz had a sister with her family living just on the other side of the wires.
Her home was on the other side, near my sister's home.
And when I walked in, but a few days before, to her, to talk it over.
Maybe she can hide us somewhere with a baby.
She said, the only thing is where is open, among the cows, the horses, but not to close  it in, not to lock us in, not to hide us in.
So then I looked at the-- at the [INAUDIBLE].
Then, when I walked there to her, I made myself that passage.
I felt a passage from the wires.
I took like two wires together, they are purple, and two wires together, the bottom part.
And this is the way how I walked over.
I passed my child to the other side, and I walked over, and we walked into a little shed.
We were there all day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But, when I came out from there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I was trying to go back, and here I got shot.
They wanted to shoot me, but they shot-- is like here.
Here is on one side, and this on the other side.
As I was holding my feet, I was thinking, they're going to shot me in the feet.
So what?
But they didn't shot me into the feets.
To my legs.
They shot straight in the baby.
And I could see now, the blood all around, the voice of my baby crying and everything.
So I ran in.
I still the power, the willpower, to remain alive was something so, so strong in me.
I don't know why.
Maybe because I shall tell the stories to my people, what we went through.
What a nation of Germany, who is now definite accepted by the-- by the world, as-- as people, as a country of murderers.
And-- Well, finish telling--
How else can we say?
Criminals.
And they are criminals.
To-- to have-- to have the strength to-- to destroy a Jewish nation, from 6 million people, this is just unbelievable.
But it happened.
And this, one of the, this was-- this was in the day, in the main [INAUDIBLE] from the-- from the-- from this [?
Lenov. ?] So he took me by my-- by my hair.
One of the Ukrainian, and, with a [INAUDIBLE] like here, in my-- to my shoulder.
[INAUDIBLE] I shall go to the [INAUDIBLE].
You know, to where-- where there was staying the trucks.
And I shall go there.
But there were so many people [INAUDIBLE] from the Jews.
They were from the bunkers, from-- from-- from the basements, and from all over.
They were running, like-- like flies.
So we want to kill off [INAUDIBLE] flies is flying away from you.
But the strength wasn't-- wasn't any stronger than the flies.
So.
But passing by, the same my sister's house, as I said.
I indicated that, you know, there was so close by.
So I-- we-- I couldn't go through the front.
The-- the doors from the back entrance as-- where his cow were, where his horse was, was, like, opened.
Come in, Molly, like somebody would call me, come in, Molly, hide yourself.
Here I-- I never even was dreaming to be there.
I never saw how it looks.
So there was wood, cutted woods, and on the woods were sacks, bags, large bags, where you will have the grain in.
But a few of them, two or three, were laying like this, empty, on the wood.
So then I, so I walked in, and I closed the door very, very hardly.
And there was like a steel-- steel stick, and I closed it from the inside.
But they probably noticed me.
But I managed to crawl, to see what there were also non-cutted wood.
And I remembered that my sister told me that there will be a barrel with-- with flour, in case winter, if we will be living so I should know that there is flour.
So I [INAUDIBLE] I-- I pulled away the one or two from those non-cutted woods, and here I noticed, thought to myself, this is a place where I can hide myself.
Maybe this will help me.
But I took that-- that-- that sack, you know, from the empty bags, and I put it in like this, all the way, inside, like you-- you-- you pulled up your-- your stocking.
All the way.
And I crawled into that barrel.
Near me.
And I took back, covered back myself with the same wood.
Like wood trees.
Not cutted.
The heavy [INAUDIBLE].
Near me was the stepladder to the roof.
When they broke the doors, they were thinking that I went-- I saw them, but they didn't see me.
They were there, there, there.
The-- the Ukrainians were-- were giving them instructions, maybe there.
But they saw me running in.
But they didn't know where.
And they walked away.
And there was a wind, and that day was, that evening was, a terrible wind.
The doors opened.
All of a sudden, when I-- as I was sitting there, I hear that-- like this.
And the wind-- and the wind.
And-- and the Ukraine were came in, and they throw it out, through the windows, things.
A lot of-- all the things, which-- belongings from my sister's.
So I thought to myself, I'll wait up.
In a wind like this, it's good to run away.
They won't to hear me.
And this is what it was.
I waited up, and I didn't hear any shooting.
Because otherwise, from the beginning on, as they were, you know, pushing the people to the trucks, there-- there was different.
But as-- as it quiet down, the went quiet down, so I took myself out slowly from that barrel.
And I crawled in back to the place, I thought to myself, here I'll see maybe my-- my baby is still alive.
I only saw the blood.
I didn't see no more of my baby.
And I went into the same, just to stay for, oh, until I shall strike myself out.
So I walked into the same little-- were I was before.
But she said I can go in there.
So I was there, and then I was thinking to myself, well, just a few minutes.
I-- I felt that-- that a-- a sharp nail was falling there into [INAUDIBLE], because they were shooting all night.
Then I thought to myself, when it will, only maybe they will get tired.
And they will fell asleep.
I had now my pain.
It's my pinch a nerve trouble.
So I got out from there, and I walked through the fields all the way to Shurtz again.
OK.
Now, after you were at Shurtz, and you said--
From--
He said to go into the woods.
So I went to the woods.
And there, where I met-- met my-- my brother-in-law, with my-- No, at Shurtz's-- Shurtz while I was there, he came also there.
I told him to come.
You know?
And he knew those people better than I, because he used to do business with them.
So-- excuse me, maybe it's--
No, no.
That's fine.
Ooh.
I have to stand up.
Ooh!
Have a cramp over here.
And we-- we were digging, we were-- excuse me, I'm sorry.
That's OK.
We were digging a bunker.
In the woods.
We got that, you know, Shurtz taught-- taught us how to do it.
Should we just go and-- and make for yourself a place.
And then, from time to-- there, not far, there is a river where we were taking our water to drink.
So this way I had an operation after the war, and I-- a bag of it with-- with sand and-- and little stones, were taking out from-- from my bladder.
And this why I had here, also, an operation from a stone, because I was drinking the dirty water from the-- from the rivers.
[SIGHS] Until after the war.
Well, until there-- there-- we got, like in May '44.
May '44 we got liberated.
We were from right after they-- after they came out from my sister's, you know.
Oh, good two years, yeah.
Good two years.
Yes.
We came to this-- this-- [?
Soltis ?] told-- told-- gave us all the time the situation, the war situation, the news.
And they gave us food.
How do you like that?
And-- and water to drink.
And they-- and a-- a-- they used to take away from us, you know, what we, the dirty-- dirty things.
You know?
And [INAUDIBLE] had a son and a daughter.
Nobody gave us out, and an old woman.
And we were there until the Russians--
Oh.
From-- from the woods, we went back again.
Not to Shurtz.
To another one.
The other one was the head of the-- of that farm.
I don't remember.
Yeah.
I don't remember.
But the other one was [? actually ?] good to us.
Very good.
And he-- like I said, once I was coughing.
And his wife came out, and she said, if you're coughing, this is very bad.
I'll make a medicine for you.
So what does she brought me to drink?
Blood, a soup.
She-- she told me, this soup comes from the liver blood from a-- from a pig.
[LAUGHS] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] And I drank that.
And that helped me.
We were ready-- we were ready then.
Let's see.
Another-- another couple.
Begged us.
No.
Another couple, a-- a brother and a sister, also went with us.
And some others went to another place.
Anyways, 14 people who were in our bunker--
From the first, until we separated, those 14 people are alive.
Most-- most of the time.
Yeah.
The other one were from--
We got liberated from that farm, and the Russians, the Russian army, found us.
They were told by that-- by that [?
Soltis ?].
Soltis ?], you know, how do I say [? a soltis ?]?
I don't know.
The head of the farm.
The head of the farm, yes.
And one-- OK.
Then when I came to-- OK.
May of '44.
Something, April, May.
End of April or May.
So then I went to work.
I went to look for a job.
I went to try to write letters all over to Russian, and throughout Poland, and to my parents' city, and the-- whatever.
And to his city to look for Harry.
And he did it the same.
And his letter was received by a cousin of my brother-in-law who was there in the woods, too.
In the same woods, but not in our bunker.
And she received, because her last name was the same last name, my father's.
You see, my brother in-law's.
And then I decided to write a letter from Russia.
In Russia, I-- I find myself a-- a hospital work.
I worked in that hospital.
And the same time, I was writing letters all over.
As I work here, so I have already [? deserved. ?] You know?
And-- and here, I-- I wrote a letter to his sister, to Shurtz's sister.
And that time, I knew the name.
I forgot, because her last name was different.
Perhaps, because she had the suitcases of mine.
I [? brother ?] [? worked ?] there.
And also, I gave them my sister's belongings.
And a lot of things.
And I told her, if anyone from the [? mandal ?] cares, will remain alive, she shall give it to them.
I still don't know who took it there.
Who cares?
You know.
I think that cousin of his took all of that.
So she answered me the letter.
She said, like we know which one is [INAUDIBLE].
I mean, like I see you don't know anything from your husband.
And I received several letters.
This what she wrote.
But she didn't indicate his-- his place of living, where in Russia?
Russia is big, large.
So I mailed her a registered letter, [NON-ENGLISH], you know, like a certified [NON-ENGLISH].
Listen, I paid for everything, because I worked, so I use to get money and this.
So I said, I begged that she should write to me a letter where in Russia.
I am in Russia.
Where in Russia?
She didn't answered.
So then I decided to take myself, to change my passport.
That I'm telling you.
Because I couldn't get out from-- from that hospital.
It was an army hospital.
I was thinking maybe my-- my husband is there.
But, so then I-- I went to the passport office.
And here I made friends with a woman, the main woman.
Although there it was a policeman, I-- I just walked.
He asked me what I want.
I said, oh, I'm waiting for my friends, this, that.
And here, I-- I walked over to her.
And I had a ring, which my mother-in-law, [NON-ENGLISH], she gave me a beautiful ring.
Like this.
And the Russian women likes-- likes jewelry.
And a special, that one had a good stone.
So I said, I would like to have your address.
I am a nurse, and I would like to come to see you.
Oy, she said, I have a cold.
And then, come, come, come Here is my address.
Oh, OK.
She--
